revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[af:Asuka-kyō]][[de:Asuka-kyō]][[en:Asuka, Yamato]][[es:Asuka-kyō]][[fa:آسوکا-کیو]][[fr:Asuka-kyō]][[hr:Asuka, Yamato]][[it:Asuka-kyō]][[ja:飛鳥京]][[ko:아스카쿄]][[pl:Asuka-kyō]][[pt:Asuka]][[ro:Asuka, Yamato]][[sh:Asuka (Yamato)]][[simple:Asuka, Yamato]][[tr:Asuka-kyō]]
{{short description|Imperial capital of Japan during the Asuka period (538–710); now in Nara Prefecture|qq=}} [[File:IshibutaiKofun.jpg|thumb|Ishibutai [[Kofun]], believed to be the burial site of [[Soga no Umako]] (figure shows scale)]] {{nihongo|'''Asuka'''|飛鳥|qq=}} was the Imperial {{ill|Capital of Japan|en|Capital of Japan|qid=Q1025961|lt=Capital of Japan|1=Capital of Japan}} during the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|qid=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}} (538 – 710 AD), which takes its name from this place. It is located in the present-day village of {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|qid=Q752397|lt=Asuka, Nara Prefecture|1=Asuka, Nara}}. ==Etymology== Some of the many theories of what the place was named after include the bird [[:en:common crossbill|common crossbill]], or ''isuka'' in Japanese, or local geological features, e.g. 洲処 (''suka'', meaning sandbar, sandbank or delta) or 崩地 (''asu'') + 処 (''ka'').<ref>Ikeda Suenori 池田末則, Yokota Kenichi 横田健一 et al. "飛鳥 (Asuka)" ''Nara-ken no chimei'' 奈良県の地名 Heibonsha 平凡社, 1981, p. 263.</ref>{{Clarify|date=November 2016|qq=}} Or it may have been named in honor of Asuka (or Ashuku) Nyorai, the Japanese equivalent of [[Akshobhya]], one of the Five Buddhas of Wisdom, who is still worshiped in the {{ill|Asuka-dera|en|Asuka-dera|qid=Q779755|lt=Asuka-dera|1=Asuka-dera}} (Asuka Temple), the Asuka-niimasu-jinja (the shrine for his manifestation as a Shinto god), and several other structures from those days.{{Citation needed|date= March 2007|qq=}} ==Archaeology== {{ill|Archaeology|en|Archaeology|qid=Q23498|lt=Archaeology|1=Archaeology}} projects continue to uncover relics from these ruins. Recent discoveries in the area include {{ill|Wadōkaichin|en|Wadōkaichin|qid=Q3565040|lt=Wado coins|1=Wadōkaichin}}, believed to be some of the oldest coins in Japan, and paintings in the [[Kitora Tomb|Kitora]] and [[Takamatsuzuka Tomb|Takamatsuzuka]] [[Kofun]], or tombs. The [[Ishibutai Kofun]] is also located in Asuka. On March 12, 2004, the discovery of the remains of a residence's main building adjacent to the kofun was announced. It is likely that the residence belonged to [[Soga no Umako]], who is believed to have been entombed in the kofun. ==Access== Asuka can be reached from either {{ill|Okadera Station|en|Okadera Station|qid=Q7081721|lt=Okadera Station|1=Okadera Station}} or {{ill|Asuka Station|en|Asuka Station|qid=Q4811860|lt=Asuka Station|1=Asuka Station}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|qid=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu|1=Kintetsu Railway}} train line, or by car on Route 169. == Imperial palaces == [[File:Plan of Oharida Palace.jpg|thumb|Layout of Oharida Palace]] In the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|qid=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}}, various palaces were constructed for each monarch. As soon as one emperor died, the whole court moved to a newly constructed palace, since it was considered dangerous and ominous to remain in a place where a deceased monarch's spirit might reside. Sometimes even during a single emperor's reign, palaces were changed multiple times due to destruction by fire or ill omens. Since these palaces were entirely constructed from wood, none of them have survived, although some archaeological work in modern times has uncovered such remains as stone bases for pillars. Sakurai was briefly the {{ill|Capital of Japan|en|Capital of Japan|qid=Q1025961|lt=Capital of Japan|1=Capital of Japan}} during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|en|Emperor Ingyō|qid=Q349258|lt=Emperor Ingyō|1=Emperor Ingyō}}. The life of the Imperial court was centered at the Palace of ''Tohotsu'' where the emperor lived in 457–479.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 11.</ref> Other emperors also built palaces at Asuka, including * ''Chikatsu-Asuka-Yatsuri'' Palace, 485–487<ref>{{ill|Louis Frédéric|en|Louis Frédéric|qid=Q726040|lt=Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric|1=Louis Frédéric}}. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA59&dq= "Asuka,"] ''Japan Encyclopedia'' at p. 59.</ref> in reign of {{ill|Emperor Kenzō|en|Emperor Kenzō|qid=Q360966|lt=Emperor Kenzō|1=Emperor Kenzō}}<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 14.</ref> * ''Shikishima no Kanasashi'' Palace, 540–571<ref>Koch, W. (1904). [https://archive.org/details/japangeschichte00kochgoog/page/n212 ''Japan; Geschichte nach japanischen Quellen und ethnographische Skizzen. Mit einem Stammbaum des Kaisers von Japan,'' p. 13].</ref> in reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|qid=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei|1=Emperor Kinmei}}<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 17.</ref> * ''Toyura'' Palace or ''Toyura-no-miya'', 593–603<ref name="asuka">Asuka Historical Museum, [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAMIYA/miyahensen.html Palaces of the Asuka Period,"] 1995; retrieved 2011-11-25.</ref> in the reign of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|qid=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}}<ref name="ponsonby20">Ponsonby-Fane, p. 20.</ref> * ''Oharida'' Palace or ''Oharida-no-miya'', 603–629<ref name="asuka"/> in the Suiko's reign<ref name="ponsonby20"/> * ''Okamoto'' Palace or ''Okamoto-no-miya'', 630–636<ref name="asuka"/> in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Jomei|en|Emperor Jomei|qid=Q329761|lt=Emperor Jomei|1=Emperor Jomei}}<ref name="ponsonby21">Ponsonby-Fane, p. 21.</ref> * ''Tanaka'' Palace, 636–40 * ''Umayasaka'' Palace, 640 In 640–642, the Imperial court briefly moved to the ''Kudara'' Palace in {{ill|Kōryō, Nara|en|Kōryō, Nara|qid=Q1346830|lt=Kōryō, Nara|1=Kōryō, Nara}}; then the emperor returned to Asuka where he lived at * ''Oharida'' Palace, 642–643 * ''Itabuki'' Palace or ''Itabuki no miya'', 643–645<ref name="asuka"/> in the reign of {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|en|Empress Kōgyoku|qid=Q235296|lt=Empress Kōgyoku|1=Empress Kōgyoku}}<ref name="ponsonby21"/> In 645–654, the court moved to the {{ill|Naniwa Nagara-Toyosaki Palace|en|Naniwa Nagara-Toyosaki Palace|qid=Q11659570|lt=''Naniwa Nagara-Toyosaki'' Palace|1=Naniwa Nagara-Toyosaki Palace}} in {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|qid=Q35765|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka}}; then the capital moved back to Asuka when the emperor lived at * ''Itabuki'' Palace, 655–655<ref name="asuka"/> in the reign of [[Emperor Kōtoku]]<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 23.</ref> * ''Kawahara'' Palace or ''Kawahara-no-miya'', 655–655<ref name="asuka"/> * ''Okamoto'' Palace or ''Nochi no Asuka-Okamoto-no-miya'', 656–660<ref name="asuka"/> in the reign of [[:en:Empress Saimei|Empress Saimei]]<ref name="ponsonby24">Ponsonby-Fane, p. 24.</ref> In 661–667, the court moved to the ''Tachibana no Hironiwa'' Palace (661–67) in {{ill|Asakura, Fukuoka|en|Asakura, Fukuoka|qid=Q720625|lt=Asakura, Fukuoka|1=Asakura, Fukuoka}}. Then the court moved again to the ''Ōmi'' Palace or ''Ōtsu'' Palace (667–72) in Ōmi-kyō (today [[:en:Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu, Shiga]]). Once more, the court moved back to Asuka at * ''Kiyomihara'' Palace or ''Kiomihara-no-miya'', 672–694<ref name="asuka"/> in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en|Emperor Tenmu|qid=Q335181|lt=Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 26.</ref> and in the reign of {{ill|Empress Jitō|en|Empress Jitō|qid=Q232026|lt=Empress Jitō|1=Empress Jitō}}<ref name="ponsonby27">Ponsonby-Fane, p. 27.</ref> Asuka was abandoned by {{ill|Empress Jitō|en|Empress Jitō|qid=Q232026|lt=Empress Jitō|1=Empress Jitō}} when she and her court moved to {{ill|Fujiwara-kyō|en|Fujiwara-kyō|qid=Q1196381|lt=Fujiwara-kyō|1=Fujiwara-kyō}}. ==See also== * {{ill|The 100 Views of Nature in Kansai|en|The 100 Views of Nature in Kansai|qid=Q11656381|lt=The 100 Views of Nature in Kansai|1=The 100 Views of Nature in Kansai}} ==External links== * Asuka Historical Museum: [http://www.nabunken.go.jp/asuka/index.html], exterior view == References == {{refbegin|qq=}} *{{cite book|last= Ponsonby-Fane|first= Richard|author-link= Richard Ponsonby-Fane|title= The Imperial Family of Japan|year= 1915|location= Kobe|oclc= 9113902|qq=}} {{refend|qq=}} {{Reflist|qq=}} {{s-start|qq=}} {{s-new|First|qq=}} {{s-ttl|title=Capital of Japan|years=538–710|qq=}} {{s-aft|after={{ill|Fujiwara-kyō|en|Fujiwara-kyō|qid=Q1196381|lt=Fujiwara-kyō|1=Fujiwara-kyō}}|qq=}} {{s-end|qq=}} {{Imperial Palaces Japan|qq=}} {{coord|34|28|56|N|135|48|47|E|display=title|region:JP-29_type:landmark_source:dewiki|qq=}}
[[en:Asuka, Yamato]]
